title: Optimum-Transmission Free-Space Optical Communications
authors: Prajit Dhara, Babak N. Saif, Jeffrey H. Shapiro, Saikat Guha
abstract: Slepian developed the Prolate Spheroidal Wavefunction (PSW) spatial-mode basis, which forms the normal modes of the Fresnel-propagation kernel of a free-space optical communications channel bookended by hard-circular apertures. The zero-th order PSW mode has the highest power-transfer eigenvalue, exciting which on the transmitter side therefore maximizes the transmissivity for single-spatial-mode communications. We show that the transmissivity performance of this fundamental PSW mode can be obtained by an aperture-truncated Gaussian beam of an optimized beam waist, despite the two mode shapes deviating from one another in the near-field regime.
summary: This paper addresses the efficiency of light transmission in free-space optical communication. It demonstrates that while Prolate Spheroidal Wavefunctions are the mathematically optimal modes for aperture-limited channels, standard Gaussian beams with optimized widths can achieve identical transmission performance. Furthermore, the authors provide a fundamental theorem stating that the total capacity of the channel is determined solely by the Fresnel number product, regardless of the specific aperture shapes.
main_problem: Determining the performance gap between theoretically optimal Prolate Spheroidal Wavefunction (PSW) modes and practical Gaussian beams in aperture-limited free-space optical communication links.
main_result: The paper proves that an aperture-truncated Gaussian beam with an optimized waist achieves the same transmissivity as the fundamental PSW mode, and derives a trace theorem showing total modal transmissivity depends only on the Fresnel number product.
method: The authors use Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) of the Fresnel propagation kernel, numerical optimization of beam waist parameters, and analytical derivation of a trace theorem for the power-transfer operator.
model_or_system: A monochromatic, scalar, paraxial optical field propagating through a free-space link between two hard circular apertures (transmitter and receiver) over a distance L.
key_observables: Transmissivity (eta), input efficiency (eta_in), and total modal power transmissivity (sum of eigenvalues).
important_parameters: Fresnel number product (Df), beam waist (xi), and aperture radii ratios (rT:rR); propagation regimes include near-field, intermediate-field, and far-field.
main_assumptions: Paraxial approximation, monochromatic field, vacuum propagation, and hard-edged circular apertures.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the propagation geometry; Figure 2 plots modal transmissivity vs Df; Figure 6 shows mode occupation statistics; Figure 7 compares input efficiency; Figures 8-10 illustrate the evolution of beam intensity profiles across different propagation regimes.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the physical system and the PSW basis, moves to comparing Gaussian beams with PSW modes via numerical optimization, derives a trace theorem for total transmissivity, and concludes with an analysis of beam evolution across different Fresnel regimes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
